title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an astronomer called Marcus talking about evening sessions for young people at an observatory. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an astronomer called Marcus talking about evening sessions for young people at an observatory. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: An astronomer called Marcus introduces evening sessions at an observatory.
extracts.audio_script: Good evening. My name's Marcus, and I organise the youth programme at Starview Observatory. The observatory stands on a hill outside town, where street lighting is less likely to affect what we can see in the sky. People are often surprised by its shape because the oldest part was not designed for astronomy. It began as a [9]water tower[/9], then was converted when a local scientific society needed a suitable place for its telescope. Modern teaching rooms and an outdoor viewing area have since been built beside it.||Students joining our programme attend one evening each month. No previous knowledge is required, and we begin with objects that are easy to recognise. Many young people hope to see distant planets immediately, but our first observation session concentrates on the [10]moon[/10], because its craters and shadows can be seen clearly through relatively simple equipment. You do not need to bring a telescope. However, everybody should carry a [11]red torch[/11], rather than an ordinary white one, because red light allows people to see pathways without making it difficult for their eyes to adjust to darkness.||Weather is always a challenge for astronomy. We cannot promise clear skies on the evening of every meeting. Before leaving home, participants should check the website, where the day's expected [12]cloud cover[/12] is posted by late afternoon. If there is too much cloud to observe the sky, sessions still happen indoors. On clear nights, small groups take turns using equipment on the [13]telescope platform[/13], an open space with safe barriers and enough room for instructors to supervise students. Other groups work inside until their turn arrives. Warm coats are essential, even in spring.||Indoor sessions are not simply a replacement for bad weather. Once a term we arrange a special [14]planetarium show[/14], using projected images to demonstrate how the night sky changes through the year. It has always proved popular with parents as well as students. There is also a friendly competition. Instead of an exam, participants will take part in an [15]astronomy quiz[/15], working in teams to recognise constellations and answer questions from sessions. The winners will receive books, but the main point is to review what everybody has learned in an enjoyable way.||The most exciting opportunity this year comes in August. If conditions are suitable, we will hold a late-night viewing session for the annual [16]meteor shower[/16], when many bright lines may be seen crossing the sky. Students who attend six monthly meetings will receive an [17]observation certificate[/17], recording the skills and topics they have covered. Finally, our current telescope is excellent for groups but cannot produce detailed photographs. We are therefore collecting donations for a new [18]digital camera[/18] that can be connected to it. Our fundraising lecture is next month, and families are welcome. Registration forms for the youth programme can be collected from the front desk tonight.

dialogue.speaker: marcus
dialogue.text: Good evening. My name's Marcus, and I organise the youth programme at Starview Observatory. The observatory stands on a hill outside town, where street lighting is less likely to affect what we can see in the sky. People are often surprised by its shape because the oldest part was not designed for astronomy. It began as a water tower, then was converted when a local scientific society needed a suitable place for its telescope. Modern teaching rooms and an outdoor viewing area have since been built beside it.||Students joining our programme attend one evening each month. No previous knowledge is required, and we begin with objects that are easy to recognise. Many young people hope to see distant planets immediately, but our first observation session concentrates on the moon, because its craters and shadows can be seen clearly through relatively simple equipment. You do not need to bring a telescope. However, everybody should carry a red torch, rather than an ordinary white one, because red light allows people to see pathways without making it difficult for their eyes to adjust to darkness.||Weather is always a challenge for astronomy. We cannot promise clear skies on the evening of every meeting. Before leaving home, participants should check the website, where the day's expected cloud cover is posted by late afternoon. If there is too much cloud to observe the sky, sessions still happen indoors. On clear nights, small groups take turns using equipment on the telescope platform, an open space with safe barriers and enough room for instructors to supervise students. Other groups work inside until their turn arrives. Warm coats are essential, even in spring.||Indoor sessions are not simply a replacement for bad weather. Once a term we arrange a special planetarium show, using projected images to demonstrate how the night sky changes through the year. It has always proved popular with parents as well as students. There is also a friendly competition. Instead of an exam, participants will take part in an astronomy quiz, working in teams to recognise constellations and answer questions from sessions. The winners will receive books, but the main point is to review what everybody has learned in an enjoyable way.||The most exciting opportunity this year comes in August. If conditions are suitable, we will hold a late-night viewing session for the annual meteor shower, when many bright lines may be seen crossing the sky. Students who attend six monthly meetings will receive an observation certificate, recording the skills and topics they have covered. Finally, our current telescope is excellent for groups but cannot produce detailed photographs. We are therefore collecting donations for a new digital camera that can be connected to it. Our fundraising lecture is next month, and families are welcome. Registration forms for the youth programme can be collected from the front desk tonight.
